compiled_state.title: [agent-workflow]: destructive git ops (checkout HEAD --, stash drop after partial pop) silently discard uncommitted multi-file refactor work -> hours of work lost per occurrence
compiled_state.author: Test
compiled_state.description:
## Incident Overview

**Scenario Type**: Agent workflow defect / documentation gap. Two distinct work-loss incidents in a single /skill-refactor session on 2026-04-27 (worktree-20260427-113423), each costing approximately 1.5 to 3 hours of in-progress structural-refactor work to redo. Same root pattern in both cases: destructive git operations on a working tree containing extensive uncommitted multi-file edits, with no recovery path because the work was never committed and was not protected by a backup capture.

### Expected Behavior

When an agent is mid-refactor with substantial uncommitted edits across many files (a structural-rewrite pass on a SKILL.md plus cross-skill referrers, plus tests), the workflow should either:

1. Prevent the agent from running known-destructive git commands (`git checkout HEAD -- <file>`, `git stash drop` after a partial-conflict pop) without an explicit safety check, OR
2. Provide clear, agent-discoverable documentation that names the safe-recovery pattern (e.g., 'capture `git diff > /tmp/session-<id>.patch` before any destructive operation', or 'commit WIP to a temp branch before invoking git stash with substantial uncommitted work'), OR
3. Both.

Currently neither protection is in place.

### Actual Behavior — Loss #1 (Phase 7 renumbering corruption)

**Context**: Agent had completed approximately 30 file edits in working tree (SKILL.md restructure: Empirical Validation extraction, named-agent-dispatch refs, Gate 2 family consolidation, sub-agent context compression, dispatch-context table consolidation, route-escalation script integration, worktree-isolation paragraph consolidation, trailing-Escalation-Triggers removal, plus cross-skill referrer updates in 8+ files, plus test updates in 5+ files). Diff was approximately +192/-2236 lines across 50+ files. None committed.

**Trigger**: Agent ran a Python script to apply Phase 7 renumbering (Step 0.5 -> Phase A Step 2, Step 1.5 -> Phase B Step 1, etc.). The substitution pass corrupted SKILL.md headers (`### Phase A Step 3: Check Known Issues` instead of `### Step 1: Check Known Issues`) due to a placeholder-method bug — the inline-substitution pass remapped freshly-renamed step labels because the headers had already been written with their target labels.

**Recovery attempt**: Agent ran `git checkout HEAD -- plugins/dso/skills/fix-bug/SKILL.md` to 'revert the corrupted Phase 7 attempt'. This was the destructive op.

**Result**: SKILL.md reverted to HEAD (1411 lines, original content). All structural rewrites done BEFORE the Phase 7 attempt — empirical-validation extraction, gate 2 family consolidation, sub-agent context compression, etc. — were lost along with the corrupted renumbering. Agent had to write a Python replay script to redo the structural rewrites in one combined pass plus a fresh renumbering pass with corrected placeholder discipline.

**Time cost**: approximately 1.5 hours.

### Actual Behavior — Loss #2 (stash pop drop)

**Context**: Agent had reapplied all the Loss #1 work via the replay script and added the gate-rename pass on top. Working tree now contained approximately 50 modified/new/renamed files. None committed.

**Trigger**: Agent needed to merge origin/main to pick up upstream commits potentially fixing pre-existing test failures. Sequence executed:

```
git stash push --keep-index -m "skill-refactor session work"
git merge origin/main --no-edit
git stash pop
```

The pop produced 3-way conflicts on 3 files (`investigator-base.md`, `investigator-delta-intermediate-fallback.md`, `test-reviewer-agent-review-tier.sh`).

**Recovery attempt**: Agent ran `git checkout HEAD -- <each-of-the-3-conflicted-files>` to keep the HEAD (post-merge) version. This appeared to resolve the conflicts. Agent then ran `git stash drop` to clean up the (still-listed) stash entry.

**Result**: The drop discarded silently. Diff vs HEAD afterwards showed only 24 files — the 24 from BEFORE Loss #1 work was redone. The session's structural rewrites of SKILL.md (1203 lines down from 1411), CLAUDE.md gate-rename, debug-everything cross-skill refs, contracts updates, and gate rename across 16 files were all gone.

The exact failure mode is unclear: stash pop with partial conflicts SHOULD apply non-conflicting changes, then leave conflict markers on the conflicted files. Agent ran git checkout HEAD -- on the 3 conflicted ones, expecting to resolve the conflicts only. But `git diff HEAD --name-only` after the pop showed only the 24 files of pre-Loss-#1 state, not the 50+ files of post-Loss-#1 state. Either the pop never fully applied, or some other mechanism reverted the non-conflict files. Agent dropped the stash before this was diagnosed.

**Time cost**: approximately 3 hours.

### Reproduction

Reproduction is probabilistic but the structural pattern is reliable:

```bash
# Setup: a worktree with substantial in-progress refactor (e.g., 30+ modified files,
# 1000+ line delta), none committed.

# Loss-mode A — git checkout HEAD destroys work:
git checkout HEAD -- some/critical/file.md
# All working-tree changes to that file are gone, irretrievable except via stash/reflog.

# Loss-mode B — stash pop with conflicts + checkout HEAD on conflicts + drop:
git stash push --keep-index
git merge origin/main  # (or any operation that brings in upstream changes)
git stash pop
# Conflicts reported on N files. Agent runs:
git checkout HEAD -- <conflicted-file-1>
git checkout HEAD -- <conflicted-file-2>
git checkout HEAD -- <conflicted-file-3>
# Apparent resolution. Agent then runs:
git stash drop
# Working tree now contains substantially less than expected.
# Compare diff vs HEAD before stash to diff vs HEAD now — files silently un-applied.
```

The exact mechanism for Loss-mode B is what the investigation needs to nail down. Hypotheses:

1. `git stash pop` with conflicts performs a 3-way merge per file. When the agent ran `git checkout HEAD --` on each conflicted file before the pop's internal state was cleared, git considered the pop incomplete and rolled back the non-conflict applies as well. (This contradicts standard git docs but would explain the observation.)
2. The pop succeeded for non-conflict files but the agent's mental model of the working-tree state was wrong — the 24 files visible afterwards were actually a subset that wasn't in the stash, and the structural rewrites had been applied to FILES that were also in the stash and subsequently mark-resolved-by-checkout-HEAD-during-the-pop-confusion.
3. A pre-commit hook or other PreBash hook intervened during one of the operations and reverted the file state without surfacing the action.

### Root Cause Hypothesis

Two distinct root causes share a single enabling condition: **uncommitted multi-file refactor work in the agent's working tree, with no documented or hook-enforced safe-recovery pattern**.

CLAUDE.md has guidance for user-affecting destructive operations (rm -rf, git push --force, etc.) but does NOT name 'protect your own in-flight work before destructive recovery operations' as a discipline. The skill-refactor SKILL.md prescribes a multi-file refactor sequence (8 phases, often touching 50+ files) without warning about the loss-window: phases 4-7 commonly produce 30+ uncommitted modifications BEFORE phase 8 commits. A mid-phase recovery from a corrupted substitution pass currently has no preserve-work pattern to lean on, leaving `git checkout HEAD --` as an attractive (and destructive) option.

### Investigation Plan

1. **Reproduce Loss-mode B** in isolation: create a fixture worktree with N modified files, stash, merge a branch that conflicts on K of them, pop, run `git checkout HEAD --` on each conflict, drop. Verify whether non-conflict modifications survive. Document the exact behavior of `git stash pop` after partial `git checkout HEAD` on conflicted entries.

2. **Quantify the gap in CLAUDE.md**: document the prescribed safe-recovery pattern. Recommended pattern (to be validated):
   - Before any destructive git op: `git diff > /tmp/<session-id>-pre-<op>.patch` (and the staged equivalent).
   - For mid-refactor merges: commit WIP to a throwaway branch first; do not stash multi-file refactor work.
   - For corrupted-substitution recovery: `git diff > /tmp/<session-id>.patch` first, THEN `git checkout HEAD -- <file>`, THEN `patch -p1 < /tmp/<session-id>.patch` if you want to retry.

3. **Add a CLAUDE.md rule**: under 'Executing actions with care', add a sub-rule covering protect-your-own-work for destructive git ops. Example wording: 'Before `git checkout HEAD -- <file>`, `git reset --hard`, `git stash drop`, or any operation that discards uncommitted working-tree state, capture the diff to /tmp/<session-id>.patch first. The reflog protects committed history; nothing protects uncommitted multi-file work in progress.'

4. **Optionally add a PreBash guard hook** that detects `git checkout HEAD --` and `git stash drop` patterns AND a working tree with non-trivial uncommitted changes (e.g., diff > 200 lines or > 10 files), and prompts the agent to capture a backup patch first.

5. **Update skill-refactor SKILL.md Phase 7**: add an explicit Phase 7.0 step: 'Before applying the renumbering pass, run `git diff > /tmp/<session-id>-pre-renumber.patch` so the substitution can be undone via `git checkout HEAD -- <skill-file>` followed by `patch -p1 < /tmp/<session-id>-pre-renumber.patch` if the pass produces corrupted output.'

### Severity Justification

Priority 1: each occurrence costs roughly half-a-session of work (1.5 to 3 hours). The pattern recurs whenever an agent runs a multi-phase refactor that crosses 50+ files — exactly the use case skill-refactor and large bug-cascade fixes are designed for. Two occurrences in one session is not anomalous; it is what the current workflow's lack of guardrails predicts.

### Related Tickets / Memories

- Feedback memory `feedback_no_gate_threshold_bypass.md` (saved this session) covers a different bypass pattern but reinforces the principle: when a gate fires, address the failure, do not weaken the gate. The same principle here: when a corrupted substitution fires, capture the work first, then attempt recovery.
- This session's three commits (`2e27f2f101`, `7864197284`, `dad7531610`) represent the eventual successful merge of the work; the lost intermediate states are not in any reflog.
- Bug `00a5-548f` (pre-commit hash instability) is a related but distinct workflow defect from the same session.

comments.body:
Fixed: Added rule 17 to CLAUDE.md 'Always Do These' section:

'Before any destructive git op on a working tree with uncommitted changes, capture a patch first. Run git diff > /tmp/session-wip-$(date +%s).patch before git checkout HEAD -- <file>, git reset --hard, git stash drop, or any op that discards uncommitted state.'

This addresses both loss-mode A (git checkout HEAD -- corrupting partial refactor) and loss-mode B (stash drop after conflicted pop) by requiring agents to capture a diff patch before any destructive git operation. The patch-then-revert pattern is the only recovery path for uncommitted work (not in reflog).

The optional PreBash guard hook is deferred — adding a safeguard file requires explicit per-session user approval per CLAUDE.md rule 22.

No skill-refactor SKILL.md exists in the skills directory; the Phase 7 guidance improvement is out of scope here.
comments.author: Test
